study_notes.title: Muscle Action: The Power of Antagonistic Pairs
study_notes.introduction: Muscles are capable of generating force only through contraction (shortening). Because they cannot actively push, they must work in teams called 'antagonistic pairs' to move bones back and forth across a joint.

fundamental_principles.the_pull_rule: Muscles can only pull on bones; they are physically incapable of pushing a bone back into its resting position.
fundamental_principles.antagonistic_pairs: Two muscles that work in opposition. When the prime mover (flexor) contracts to bend a joint, its partner (extensor) must relax to allow the movement.

muscle_states.contracted: The muscle is shortened, thickened, and hardened.
muscle_states.relaxed: The muscle is elongated, thinned, and softened.

arm_joint_elbow.flexor: Biceps (located on the front of the upper arm).
arm_joint_elbow.extensor: Triceps (located on the back of the upper arm).
arm_joint_elbow.action_flexion: Biceps contract (bulge) to bend the elbow; Triceps relax.
arm_joint_elbow.action_extension: Triceps contract to straighten the arm; Biceps relax.

leg_joint_knee.flexor: Hamstrings (located on the back of the thigh).
leg_joint_knee.extensor: Quadriceps (located on the front of the thigh).
leg_joint_knee.action_flexion: Hamstrings contract to bend the knee (e.g., lifting the heel toward the glutes).
leg_joint_knee.action_extension: Quadriceps contract to straighten the leg (e.g., kicking a football).

anatomy_of_the_pull.tendons: The non-elastic fibrous cords that transmit the pulling force from the contracting muscle directly to the bone.
anatomy_of_the_pull.joints: The pivot point where the bone moves, allowed by the synchronized pulling of the antagonistic pair.
anatomy_of_the_pull.nerves: The nervous system coordinates this activity, ensuring one muscle is activated while the other is inhibited (relaxed) at the exact same moment.

clinical_and_physiological_context.muscle_fatigue: Occurs when muscles are overworked and oxygen delivery fails to keep up. This leads to the buildup of lactic acid, which causes painful cramps and stiffness.
clinical_and_physiological_context.strains: Injuries occurring when a muscle or tendon is overstretched or torn, usually caused by sudden, heavy lifting or forceful movement.
clinical_and_physiological_context.the_role_of_nutrition: Proteins are the essential 'body-building' food needed to repair the microscopic tears that occur in muscle fibers during heavy labor.

study_tips_for_ple: Flexor = Bends the joint; Extensor = Straightens the joint.
The Biceps is at the front (Flexor); The Triceps is at the back (Extensor).
Always remember: Muscles PULL, they never PUSH.
If you are asked why we need muscle pairs, the answer is always: 'Because one muscle can only pull in one direction, the other is needed to pull it back.'
